coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots by recycling waste graphite and impact of oxygen adsorption on enhancing photoluminescence
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Geon Jong
Yoo, Jung Hyeon
Kwon, Seok Bin
Yoo, Ho Chan
Lee, Ui Young
Kang, Byung Guk
Kang, Bong Kyun
Yoon, Dae Ho
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are fluorescent materials highly suited for biomedical applications owing to their excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. However, the chemical substances used in recent GQDs syntheses, such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and some aromatic organics, are causing severe environmental pollution. For sustainable research, we report a method for synthesizing recycled nitrogen-doped GQDs using waste graphite anode scrap (WGAS) extracted from retired lithium-ion batteries. Interestingly, increasing the inner cylinder temperature in a Couette–Taylor flow reactor from 37 to 44 °C during graphene oxide (GO) synthesis enhanced oxygen adsorption by ≈3 %. This led to a ≈ 20 % increase in photoluminescence (PL) intensity in GQDs synthesized hydrothermally. This indicates that the increased oxygen adsorption in GO enhances the density of the resulting oxidized debris, leading to a greater number of GQDs per unit volume. Our approach demonstrates an eco-friendly route to produce blue-emitting N-GQDs with enhanced optical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Recycle
Oxidation process
Graphene quantum dot
Waste graphite anode scrap

This research was supported by the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) and Korea Institute for Advancement of Technology (KIAT) through the National Advanced Strategic Industry Specialized Complex Solidarity Cooperation Support Project (Project No. RS-2024-00508534).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of (a) the CGO 35, and (b) WGAS GO 35. FTIR spectra of (c) the CGO 35, WGAS 35. Raman spectra (d) of the CGO 35, WGAS 35. (e) Photoluminescence emission spectra of CGO 35-GQDs, WGAS 35-GQDs. (Inset; photograph taken under a 365 nm UV lamp (left: WGAS 35 GQDs, right: CGO 35 GQDs)). (f) UV–vis absorption of WGAS 35 GQDs, CGO 35 GQDs; Inset shows the uv-vis absorption results from 300 to 500 nm.

HR-TEM image of (a) WGAS 35 GQDs and (b) CGO 35 GQDs. (c) HR-TEM image of zigzag edge; (a), (b), (c) Inset shows the FFT image. (d) The wide scan XPS spectra of WGAS 35 GQDs and CGO 35 GQDs. The high-resolution (e) C 1s, (f) N 1s XPS spectra of WGAS 35 GQDs and CGO 35 GQDs.

(a) Schematic of increased oxygen adsorption on graphene surface with increasing temperature. (b) XRD patterns of WGAS, WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44. (c) Raman spectra WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44 (The ID/IG ratio are 0.963, 0.986 and 1.044, respectively). (d) FTIR spectra of WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44. (e) The wide scan XPS spectra of WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44. (f) C/O ratios of WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44.

(a) Photoluminescence emission spectra of WGAS 27, 35, 44 GQDs. (b) UV–vis absorption of WGAS 27, 35, 44 GQDs. (c) Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY, excitation wavelength at 315 nm) of WGAS 27, 35, 44 GQDs. TCSPC decay curve of (d) WGAS 27, (e) WGAS 35, (f) WGAS 44 GQDs.
(a) Normalized Photoluminescence emission spectra (b) Excitation, Emission wavelength and Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of WGAS 35 GQDs.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots by recycling waste graphite and impact of oxygen adsorption on enhancing photoluminescence

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are fluorescent materials highly suited for biomedical applications owing to their excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. However, the chemical substances used in recent GQDs syntheses, such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and some aromatic organics, are causing severe environmental pollution. For sustainable research, we report a method for synthesizing recycled nitrogen-doped GQDs using waste graphite anode scrap (WGAS) extracted from retired lithium-ion batteries. Interestingly, increasing the inner cylinder temperature in a Couette–Taylor flow reactor from 37 to 44 °C during graphene oxide (GO) synthesis enhanced oxygen adsorption by ≈3 %. This led to a ≈ 20 % increase in photoluminescence (PL) intensity in GQDs synthesized hydrothermally. This indicates that the increased oxygen adsorption in GO enhances the density of the resulting oxidized debris, leading to a greater number of GQDs per unit volume. Our approach demonstrates an eco-friendly route to produce blue-emitting N-GQDs with enhanced optical properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional fluorescent materials derived from two-dimensional graphene, possessing an infinite exciton Bohr radius [1] and, unlike graphene, a non-zero bandgap, with the dimensions scaled down to the nanometer range. As a result, GQDs have garnered significant interest in recent years owing to their edge and quantum confinement effects, endowing them with tunable band gaps. GQDs are being applied in various fields such as fluorescence [2], biosensing [3], electrocatalysis [4], photocatalysis [5], and solar cells [6]. In particular, GQDs are biocompatible, highly stable, and have low cellular toxicity and tunable band gaps, making them highly suitable for biomedical applications such as bioimaging [7], targeted imaging [8], and drug delivery [9].

Despite the remarkable performance of GQDs, several obstacles impede their commercialization. The use of certain carbon sources in the synthesis of GQDs is associated with environmental pollutants such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and some aromatic organics. To ensure sustainable research that does not contribute to environmental pollution, finding environmentally friendly and recyclable carbon sources is essential [10]. To achieve this, recent studies have focused on synthesizing materials using custard apple peel waste biomass [11], wood charcoal [12], rice husk [13], and sugarcane bagasse [14], among others. Recently, the use of lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles has increased owing to their high energy density and excellent performance characteristics, such as low power loss owing to self-discharge [15–17]. As a result, the number of discarded lithium-ion batteries is increasing and is projected to grow exponentially each year. Therefore, research on the recycling of retired lithium-ion batteries is urgently needed. In response, Kang et al. [18] extracted waste graphite anode scrap (WGAS) from discarded lithium-ion batteries and synthesized easily recyclable graphene oxide (GO). The current standardized model of GO is known to consist of two components: lightly functionalized graphene and small, highly oxidized debris (ODs) [19,20]. ODs are formed during the synthesis of GO and are adsorbed on the surface of GO, which can be separated under alkaline conditions [21,22]. In their adsorbed state on GO, ODs are amorphous; however, during the hydrothermal synthesis process under alkaline conditions, they become crystalline, thereby forming GQDs [21].

In this study, we confirmed that the degree of oxygen adsorption in the GO production process using a Couette–Taylor flow reactor, after extracting graphite from WGAS, can be adjusted by controlling the synthesis temperature. The amount of oxygen adsorbed is related to the OD yield, which is directly linked to the product yield of GQDs. As a result, we observed an ≈20 % improvement in the GQDs product yield by adjusting the temperature of the Couette–Taylor flow reactor. Additionally, the synthesized GQDs exhibit solvent-dependent photoluminescence (PL) characteristics owing to various solvents. This process has the potential to contribute to environmental remediation through the recycling of discarded materials and is expected to enhance the applicability of GQDs in next-generation applications. Understanding the role of synthesis conditions in determining the structural and optical properties of GQDs is crucial for advancing their practical applications. However, previous studies have primarily focused on varying precursor sources or post-synthesis modifications rather than controlling synthesis parameters to directly influence GQD formation. To the best of our knowledge, no previous study has established a direct correlation between the synthesis temperature of GO, oxygen adsorption, and the yield of GQDs derived from WGAS. Our findings provide a new strategy for optimizing eco-friendly GQD production.

GO nanosheets were synthesized using WGAS (GeneSung Materials Co. Ltd., Korea) through a modified Couette–Taylor flow reactor (Laminar Co. Ltd., Korea). Initially, WGAS (7.8 g) was added to 275 mL of sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95 %) and stirred for 10 min. Following this, 39 g of potassium permanganate (KMnO4) was gradually introduced into the mixture while maintaining the solution in an ice bath. After stirring and cooling the mixture for 40 min, the dark green solution was transferred into the gap between two concentric cylinders. The inner cylinder was rotated at 1400 rpm for 1.5 h, with a reaction conducted at temperatures of 27, 35, and 44 °C. The samples synthesized at these temperatures are labeled as WGAS GO (WGAS graphene oxide) 27, 35, and 44, respectively. After 90 min, 800 mL of dilute sulfuric acid (5 %) was carefully added and stirred into the mixture, allowing it to react for an additional 2 h. Subsequently, 24 mL of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 34.5 %) was added, and the mixture reacted for an additional 30 min before being washed three times at 7000 rpm. During this process, the mixture's color quickly shifted to a bright yellow. The suspension was further washed three times with a mixture of 1 L of deionized water, 30 mL of diluted sulfuric acid (5 %), and 5 mL of hydrogen peroxide (30 %) to remove residual metal ions. Then, it underwent five cycles of centrifugation at 8500 rpm with deionized water. The resulting graphite oxide (GtO) was rinsed with purified water until neutral pH was achieved, followed by freeze-drying. To obtain GO solids, the GtO powders were dispersed in water, and sonicated for 1 h. Afterward, the GO suspension was centrifuged and freeze-dried, producing brown GO powders.

The synthesis was conducted using the same method as described above, with the exception of the starting material. Specifically, WGAS was replaced with commercial GO (commercial graphene oxide) while maintaining all other experimental parameters identical. To compare commercial graphite with WGAS, the reaction was carried out only at 35 °C. The samples synthesized using commercial graphite are labeled as CGO 35.

N-doped GQDs were easily synthesized using a hydrothermal method. First, WGAS GO (0.07 g) and CGO were dispersed in 70 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 1 h. Then, an Aqueous ammonia solution (28 wt%, 0.7 mL) was added to the GO solution, and the resulting solution was sonicated for 10 min and transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the black reduced graphene oxide (rGO) precipitate was separated by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min). Any remaining rGO in the solution was collected by filtration through a 0.22 µm membrane filter. The excess ammonia solution was then evaporated in an 80 °C oil bath. Finally, the solution was freeze dried to obtain an ivory-colored N-doped GQDs powder. The synthesized GQDs are labeled as WGAS 27, 35, 44 GQDs and CGO 35 GQDs, corresponding to the raw materials WGAS GO 27, 35, 44 and CGO 35, respectively. Fig. S1 shows images of the synthesized GQDs under daylight and UV light. Fig. S1(a)–(d) shows images of WGAS 27, 35, and 44 GQDs, and CGO 35 GQDs under daylight, while Fig. S1(e)-(f) presents images of WGAS 27, 35, and 44 GQDs, and CGO 35 GQDs under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The surface morphology of WGAS GO and CGO was observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM; JSM-7600F, JEOL). The oxygen adsorption of WGAS GO was evaluated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; ESCA 2000, Multilab). The chemical bonding of each sample was analyzed using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; IS 10, Sinco). Raman spectra were obtained using a micro-Raman spectrometer (Bruker FRA 160/S, Bruker) with an excitation energy of 2.41 eV (514 nm). The structure and lattice spacing of WGAS GO were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD; Bruker D8 FOCUS) with a Cu-Kα radiation source. The GQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100F). PL and PL excitation were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FluoroMate FS-2, Sinco). The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was determined using a quantum efficiency measurement system (QE-2100, Otsuka). The absorbance of the GQDs was measured using a UV–Vis–near infrared (NIR) spectrophotometer (Cary5000, Agilent). Time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) measurements were carried out using a confocal microscope (MicroTime-200, Picoquant, Germany).

Fig. 1 compares CGO 35 (commercial GO, sourced from commercial Alpha Graphite; synthesis temperature: 35 °C) and WGAS GO 35 (sourced from WGAS; synthesis temperature: 35 °C), both synthesized under the same conditions using a Couette–Taylor flow reactor. SEM images of WGAS GO 35 and CGO 35 synthesized using the Couette–Taylor flow reactor are shown in Fig. 1(a) and (b), respectively, and reveal the typical surface morphology of GO. Fig. 1(c) shows the FTIR spectra of WGAS 35 and CGO 35. In both spectra, the peak at 3400 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the stretching vibration of the O–H group, while the peak at 1730 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C=O group. The peaks at 1620 cm⁻¹ and 1415 cm⁻¹ represent the O=C–OH stretching vibration, while those at 1226 cm⁻¹ and 1040 cm⁻¹ represent the C–O–C (epoxy group) and C–O (alkoxy group) stretching vibrations, respectively. These results confirm that WGAS GO 35 was successfully synthesized into GO. Fig. 1(d) shows the Raman spectra of WGAS GO 35 and CGO 35. The peak at ≈1350 cm⁻¹ represents the D band, corresponding to the breathing mode of sp²-hybridized carbon rings activated by defects [23,24]. The peak at ≈1580 cm⁻¹ represents the G band, indicating bond stretching of sp²-hybridized carbon pairs [23]. The presence of the D and G bands indicates that the graphene surface is functionalized with oxygen-containing groups, confirming the successful formation of GO. Fig. 1(e) shows the PL emission spectra of the WGAS GO 35 GQDs and CGO 35 GQDs solutions synthesized via the hydrothermal process. The ODs attached to the GO sheets detach under alkaline conditions owing to the breakdown of hydrogen bonds and the electrostatic repulsion resulting from the ionization of carboxylic acid induced by the nucleophilic attack of –OH [25]. The detached ODs are converted into crystalline GQDs through a hydrothermal process under high-temperature and high-pressure conditions [21]. The synthesized GQDs solution exhibits the strongest emission peak at 420 nm, showing intense blue luminescence consistent with previously reported studies [26]. Therefore, the PL emission spectra indicate that WGAS GO 35 was successfully synthesized into GQDs, similarly to CGO 35. Fig. 1(f) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the synthesized GQDs. Characteristic absorption peaks at ca. 230 nm and ca. 280 nm are observed in the spectra of both WGAS 35 GQDs and CGO 35 GQDs. The peak at ca. 230 nm is attributed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic C=C bond, while that at ca. 280 nm is assigned to the n→π* transition of the C=O bond [27,28]. Additionally, a characteristic absorption peak is observed at ≈360 nm, corresponding to the n→π* transition of the C–N bond [29,30]. This result suggests that ammonia solution serves as an N source, leading to the successful introduction of nitrogen into the GQDs via the hydrothermal process. Therefore, it can be concluded that WGAS GO can produce GQDs similarly to commercial graphite.

Fig. 2 shows the structural characteristics of WGAS 35 GQDs and CGO 35 GQDs. Fig. 2(a) and (b) show the high-resolution TEM images of the two types of GQDs, which confirm the successful formation of highly crystalline GQDs under hydrothermal treatment in ammonia. Fig. S2 shows that the mean diameters of WGAS 35 GQDs and CGO 35 GQDs were measured to be 4.2 nm and 4.1 nm, respectively. This indicates that temperature control during the GO synthesis stage does not affect the size of the GQDs. The lattice spacings were measured to be 0.21 nm, clearly indicating the (100) plane of graphite. Additionally, the TEM images of WGAS 27 GQDs and WGAS 44 GQDs are shown in Fig. S3, clearly showing that all GO synthesized under each condition was converted into GQDs. Fig. 2(c) shows the zigzag edges of WGAS 35 GQDs, which exhibits a triplet ground state similar to the carbene mechanism in the PL mechanisms of GQDs [31]. The carbene centers at zigzag edges are stabilized by σ–π coupling, enabling luminescence [31,32]. Therefore, nanoscale GQDs exhibit strong PL owing to the high concentration of zigzag sites. The survey scan spectrum in Fig. 2(d) demonstrates that GQDs have been successfully nitrogen-doped. Fig. 2(e) shows the high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of WGAS 35 GQDs and CGO 35 GQDs. This clearly demonstrates that the three peaks corresponding to C=C, C-O-C, and C=O represent graphite sp2-carbon, ether bonds, and carbon doubly bound to oxygen, respectively [33,34]. The peaks in the N 1s spectra of Fig. 2(f) at 397.8 eV, 399.4 eV, and 400.02 eV can be assigned to pyridinic N, NH2, and pyrrolic N, respectively [35–37]. The spectra of the synthesized GQDs exhibit distinct N 1s peaks, confirming that nitrogen doping occurred in the ammonia solution.

Previous studies have demonstrated that processing parameters, such as the rotating speeds and reaction time of the Couette–Taylor flow reactor, can affect the final oxidation efficiency of WGAS GO during the transformation of WGAS to WGAS GO [38]. This implies that the amount of oxygen introduced into WGAS GO can be controlled by external factors. Fig. 3 shows the experimental results, which reveal the relationship between the synthesis temperature and the oxidation of WGAS GO when synthesized using a Couette–Taylor flow reactor. Fig. 3(a) is a schematic showing the increase in oxygen adsorption with the rise in inner cylinder temperature of the Couette–Taylor flow reactor. The internal cylinder temperatures were adjusted to 27, 35, and 44 °C (WGAS GO 27, WGAS GO 35, WGAS GO 44, respectively) to investigate this relationship. As a result, WGAS GO 44, synthesized at the highest temperature, exhibited the greatest increase in both oxidation and interlayer spacing. As the temperature increased, the ion activity also increased, enhancing the oxidation process. This was confirmed through Raman and XPS analyses, which showed an increase in the number of oxygen functional groups on the surface of WGAS GO. Additionally, the increase in surface oxygen functional groups expanded the interlayer spacing of WGAS GO, which was confirmed by the XRD patterns, showing that the diffraction peak of WGAS GO shifted from 2θ = 10.46° to 9.46° at lower angles. Fig. 3(b) shows the XRD patterns of WGAS and WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44 (Detailed peak information is shown in Fig. S4.). Before oxidation, the XRD pattern of WGAS contained a main peak at 2θ = 26.63°, corresponding to a d(002) spacing of ≈0.34 nm, which is consistent with natural graphite [18,39]. Diffraction peaks for WGAS GO synthesized at different temperatures are located at 2θ ≈ 10°, indicating that WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44 were all successfully oxidized through the Couette–Taylor flow reactor process [18]. The diffraction peaks of WGAS GO appear at 2θ = 10.46°, 10.02°, and 9.48° for the 27, 35, and 44 °C syntheses. This suggests that as the internal cylinder temperature increases, the diffraction peaks shift to lower angles, indicating that higher synthesis temperatures expand the interlayer spacing of WGAS GO. Additionally, the reaction time can influence the final oxidation efficiency. Therefore, the correlation between reaction time and interlayer spacing is shown in Fig. S5. As the reaction time increases, the number of oxygen functional groups attached to the surface of graphite increases, leading to an expansion of the interlayer spacing. Raman spectroscopy was used to estimate the oxidation degree of WGAS GO at different temperatures (Fig. 3(c)). The ID/IG ratio calculated from the Raman spectra serves as an estimate of surface defect content and, consequently, the oxidation of WGAS GO [40]. The ID/IG ratios for WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44 were 0.963, 0.986, and 1.044, respectively, indicating an increase in oxidation with higher internal cylinder temperatures in the Couette–Taylor reactor. Fig. 3(d) shows the FTIR spectra of WGAS GO at different temperatures. To analyze the oxygen adsorption in the synthesized samples in greater detail, XPS measurements were conducted (Fig. 3(e)). However, the detected sulfur is a trace residue generated during the GO synthesis process. Fig. 3(f) compares the C/O content ratios for WGAS GO at different temperatures. It can be seen that oxygen adsorption increases with increasing temperature, and the C/O ratios of WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44 were measured to be 2.21, 2.14, and 1.89, respectively.

Fig. 4 shows data on the optical properties of WGAS GQDs solutions at different GO synthesis temperatures. According to previous reports, oxygen-containing groups in GO are primarily contained within ODs [20,22,41]. Thus, the higher oxygen content in WGAS GO observed at higher synthesis temperatures indicates a corresponding increase in ODs. Fig. 4(a) shows the PL emission spectra of GQDs solutions synthesized from WGAS GO 27, 35, and 44 through the hydrothermal process (WGAS 27, 35, 44 GQDs). The results reveal that the PL intensity of the GQDs solutions increases proportionally with the oxygen content of WGAS GO (Fig. S6 shows the PL and PL excitation curves). This suggests that a higher oxygen content leads to a greater number of ODs being converted into GQDs. The PL intensity of GQDs tends to increase as the concentration of GQDs increases [42]. Consequently, the increase in the number of GQDs per unit volume leads to an enhancement in PL intensity. Furthermore, the emission peaks for the WGAS 27, 35, and 44 GQDs are positioned at ≈420 nm, indicating similar chemical states of the GQDs. This implies that the synthesis temperature in the Couette–Taylor flow reactor is closely related to the product yield of GQDs. Fig. 4(b) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of WGAS GQDs synthesized at different temperatures. Additionally, a distinct absorption peak at ≈360 nm is observed, which corresponds to the n–π* transition of C–N bonds [29,30]. Fig. 4(c) shows the PLQY values of WGAS GQDs synthesized at varying temperatures. The measured PLQY values for the WGAS 27, 35, and 44 GQDs are 19.8 %, 20 %, and 18 %, respectively. The PLQY of GQDs can be influenced by various chemical factors, such as heteroatom doping [43] and surface engineering [44]. However, the WGAS 27, 35, and 44 GQDs exhibit nearly identical PLQY values. This observation suggests that temperature control during the synthesis of GO does not significantly affect the final PLQY of the GQDs. In other words, the synthesis conditions pertaining to the GO temperature do not alter the chemical properties of the GQDs. Consequently, the observed increase in PL intensity for WGAS 27, 35, and 44 GQDs in Fig. 4(a) can be attributed to an enhancement in the product yield, rather than changes in their intrinsic chemical characteristics. Fig. 4(d)–(f) show the TRPL analysis of WGAS GQDs solutions, which allows for the identification of the recombination mechanisms of GQDs [45] (Fig. S7 shows the data comparing the TCSPC decay curves and PLQY of WGAS 35 GQDs and CGO GQDs.). The fluorescence decay curve indicates that fluorescence decay occurs via three distinct relaxation pathways [46–48]. Eq. (1) shows the tri-exponential function [45]. Detailed carrier lifetime components (τ1, τ2, τ3), relative abundances (C1, C2, C3) are shown in Table S1.
(1)

The three different lifetimes are attributed to defects at the edge sites and functional groups of the GQDs. The shortest lifetime (τ1) is associated with the π*–π transition in isolated sp2-hybridized carbon domains, while the other lifetimes (τ2, τ3) are attributed to edge sites and defects in the functional groups of the GQDs [46]. Using Eq. (2), the average decay times were calculated to be 3.73, 3.59, and 3.731 ns for the WGAS 27, 35, and 44 GQDs, respectively, showing minimal variation. This indicates that their chemical properties are similar.
(2)

)

According to previous studies, the PL mechanism of GQDs involves various factors, including quantum confinement effects, surface state emission, edge states, heteroatom doping, and solvatochromism [49–51]. This paper provides additional evidence for the solvent-dependent PL characteristics of GQDs, which can be dissolved in many solvents owing to the presence of oxygenated functional groups. Adjusting the emission wavelength depending on the solvent is a highly valuable area of research for various applications, and studies utilizing the solvent-dependent properties of GQDs are actively ongoing. Fig. 5 shows the solvent-dependent properties of the synthesized WGAS 35 GQDs dispersed in various polar solvents. As shown in Fig. 5(a), the emission peak shifts from 400 to 440 nm, accompanied by asymmetric changes in the full width at half maximum with unpredictable trends. This suggests that the solvent environment influences the GQDs in a complex manner through non-uniform interactions. To provide a clearer comparison of these changes, the optical properties are summarized in Fig. 5(b). The factors affecting the GQDs can be broadly categorized into two solvent characteristics: polarity and hydrogen-bonding capability. Polarity typically affects the GQDs indirectly, while hydrogen bonding directly influences them through specific interactions. Based on this understanding, the emission wavelength red shifts with increasing solvent polarity. High-polarity solvents can enhance the dipole moment of the GQDs, narrowing the bandgap. However, as indicated in the graph, dimethylformamide (DMF) and ethanol (EtOH) exhibit similar emission wavelengths despite the higher polarity of DMF. This can be explained by hydrogen-bonding effects. The presence of C–N bonds on the GQDs surface induces stronger dipole–dipole interactions with surrounding molecules. Although EtOH is less polar than DMF, its greater hydrogen bonding potential allows for more active interactions with the GQDs, attracting electrons to the surface. As a result, the strongly confined electrons and holes in EtOH lose more energy before recombination, leading to a larger Stokes shift than those of less confined GQDs in non-polar solvents. Similarly, tetrahydrofuran is less polar than DMF and EtOH, and its reduced hydrogen-bonding potential causes a smaller red shift compared with these solvents. By contrast, ethyl ether, as a non-polar solvent, does not interact significantly with the GQDs. This isolation from the solvent results in stronger quantum confinement and a smaller Stokes shift. The polarity and hydrogen-bonding potential of solvents significantly influence the PLQY of GQDs, as illustrated in the bar graph in Fig. 5(b). The observed PLQY trends closely mirror the red-shift trends; however, the PLQY is predominantly governed by the hydrogen-bonding potential of the solvent rather than its polarity. Typically, the primary mechanism contributing to non-radiative recombination arises from the vibration and rotation of surface functional groups. As the results demonstrate, the PLQY varies among solvents, and this variation aligns more closely with the order of hydrogen bonding potential. Furthermore, a comparison with previous reports (Table S2) reveals that the PLQY values obtained in this study are comparable to or even exceed those previously reported, demonstrating that GQDs derived from waste graphite exhibit significant potential for practical applications and offer an alternative recycling pathway. This finding suggests that hydrogen bonding, which has a more direct effect on the surface than polarity, can effectively constrain the rotational and vibrational movements of functional groups, thereby reducing the non-radiative recombination processes in GQDs.

Using a Couette–Taylor flow reactor, we synthesized GO and recycled N-doped GQDs through hydrothermal treatment with ammonia. Our results indicate that as the reactor's internal temperature increased, oxygen adsorption on the GO surface also intensified. This increased adsorption led to the formation of ODs on the graphite surface, which subsequently influenced the experimental yield of GQDs synthesis. As a result, the GQDs product yield improved in parallel with the increased oxidation of graphite, leading to an ≈20 % enhancement in PL intensity. Furthermore, the synthesized GQDs exhibited solvent-dependent properties, modulated by the polarity and hydrogen-bonding capacity of the solvents. Based on the increase in PLQY from 11 % in ethyl ether to 27.5 % in EtOH, we conclude that hydrogen-bonding interactions between the surface functional groups of GQDs and the solvents effectively reduced non-radiative recombination processes. This study underscores the potential applications of GQDs in advanced technologies and highlights their relevance in industrial processes, such as the recycling of waste batteries. Especially, if trends from large-scale production are further validated, this could bring GQDs closer to industrial applications.

Geon Jong Yu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jung Hyeon Yoo: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Seok Bin Kwon: Investigation, Conceptualization. Ho Chan Yoo: Investigation. Ui Young Lee: Formal analysis. Byung Guk Kang: Validation, Investigation. Bong Kyun Kang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Dae Ho Yoon: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea(NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. RS-2023-00221186). This research was supported by the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (MOTIE) and Korea Institute for Advancement of Technology (KIAT) through the National Advanced Strategic Industry Specialized Complex Solidarity Cooperation Support Project (Project No. RS-2024-00508534).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Lingam, R. Podila, H.J. Qian, S. Serkiz, A.M. Rao, Evidence for edge-State photoluminescence in graphene quantum dots, Adv. Funct. Mater., 23 (2013) 5062–5065. 10.1002/adfm.201203441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anomalous behaviors of visible luminescence from graphene quantum dots: interplay between size and shape
ACS Nano

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kim, S.W. Hwang, M.K. Kim, D.Y. Shin, D.H. Shin, C.O. Kim, S.B. Yang, J.H. Park, E. Hwang, S.H. Choi, G. Ko, S. Sim, C. Sone, H.J. Choi, S. Bae, B.H. Hong, Anomalous behaviors of visible luminescence from graphene quantum dots: interplay between size and shape, ACS Nano, 6 (2012) 8203–8208. 10.1021/nn302878r.
